items.passage: Many deep-sea creatures produce their own light through a chemical reaction known as bioluminescence. This glow appears when a molecule called luciferin reacts with oxygen inside special organs in the body. Some fish use the cold blue light to attract curious prey toward their waiting jaws. Others flash bright signals to confuse predators or to find a mate in the darkness. Because sunlight never reaches these depths, such living light remains the only reliable source of brightness below.

Wetlands are among the most productive ecosystems on the planet, yet many have been drained for farming and housing. Restoration projects now work to bring water back to these damaged areas so that native plants can return. Once the soil stays wet again, frogs, birds, and insects gradually rebuild their broken communities. Healthy marshes also filter pollution from rivers and store large amounts of carbon underground. For these reasons, governments increasingly protect the wetlands that remain along their coasts.

Saturn is surrounded by a spectacular system of rings that stretches far into space yet remains remarkably thin. The rings are made of countless pieces of ice and rock, from tiny grains to chunks the size of houses. Scientists believe these fragments may be the remains of a moon that was torn apart by gravity long ago. As the particles orbit the planet, they collide gently and spread into the flat bands we observe today. Spacecraft have revealed gaps within the rings that are shaped by the pull of nearby moons.
